rows.pali: Duvidhakoṭṭhāse channaṃ anussatiṭṭhānānaṃ maraṇassatiyā upasamānussatiyā āhāre paṭikūlasaññāya catudhātuvavatthānassāti imesaṃ vasena laddhacittekaggatā, yā ca appanāsamādhīnaṃ pubbabhāge ekaggatā, ayaṃ upacārasamādhi.
rows.english: 2. In the section dealing with that of two kinds, access concentration is the unification of mind obtained by the following, that is to say, the six recollections, mindfulness of death, the recollection of peace, the perception of repulsiveness in nutriment, and the defining of the four elements, and it is the unification that precedes absorption concentration.

rows.pali: "Paṭhamassa jhānassa parikammaṃ paṭhamassa jhānassa anantarapaccayena paccayo"ti ādivacanato pana yā parikammānantarā ekaggatā, ayaṃ appanāsamādhīti evaṃ upacārappanāvasena duvidho.
rows.english: Absorption concentration is the unification that follows immediately upon the preliminary-work (IV.74) because of the words, “The first-jhāna preliminary-work is a condition, as proximity condition, for the first jhāna” (Paṭṭh II 350 (Se). So it is of two kinds as access and absorption.

rows.pali: Dutiyaduke tīsu bhūmīsu kusalacittekaggatā lokiyo samādhi.
rows.english: 7.3.In the second dyad mundane concentration is profitable unification of mind in the three planes.

rows.pali: Ariyamaggasampayuttā ekaggatā lokuttaro samādhīti evaṃ lokiyalokuttaravasena duvidho.
rows.english: Supramundane concentration is the unification associated with the noble paths. So it is of two kinds as mundane and supramundane.

rows.pali: Tatiyaduke catukkanaye dvīsu pañcakanaye tīsu jhānesu ekaggatā sappītiko samādhi.
rows.english: 8.4.In the third dyad concentration with happiness is the unification of mind in two jhānas in the fourfold reckoning and in three jhānas in the fivefold reckoning.

rows.pali: Avasesesu dvīsu jhānesu ekaggatā nippītiko samādhi.
rows.english: Concentration without happiness is the unification in the remaining two jhānas.

rows.pali: Upacārasamādhi pana siyā sappītiko, siyā nippītikoti evaṃ sappītikanippītikavasena duvidho.
rows.english: But access concentration may be with happiness or without happiness. So it is of two kinds as with happiness and without happiness.

rows.pali: Catutthaduke catukkanaye tīsu pañcakanaye catūsu jhānesu ekaggatā sukhasahagato samādhi.
rows.english: 9.5.In the fourth dyad concentration accompanied by bliss is the unification in three jhānas in the fourfold and four in the fivefold reckoning.

rows.pali: Avasesasmiṃ upekkhāsahagato samādhi.
rows.english: That accompanied by equanimity is that in the remaining jhāna.

rows.pali: Upacārasamādhi pana siyā sukhasahagato, siyā upekkhāsahagatoti evaṃ sukhasahagataupekkhāsahagatavasena duvidho.
rows.english: Access concentration may be accompanied by bliss or accompanied by equanimity. So it is of two kinds as accompanied by bliss and accompanied by equanimity.

rows.pali: Tikesupaṭhamattike paṭiladdhamatto hīno, nātisubhāvito majjhimo, subhāvito vasippatto paṇītoti evaṃ hīnamajjhimapaṇītavasena tividho.
rows.english: 10. 6. In the first of the triads what has only just been acquired is inferior. What is not very well developed is medium. What is well developed and has reached mastery is superior. So it is of three kinds as inferior, medium, and superior.

rows.pali: Dutiyattike paṭhamajjhānasamādhi saddhiṃ upacārasamādhinā savitakkasavicāro.
rows.english: 11.7.In the second triad that with applied thought and sustained thought is the concentration of the first jhāna together with access concentration.

rows.pali: Pañcakanaye dutiyajjhānasamādhi avitakkavicāramatto.
rows.english: That without applied thought, with sustained thought only, is the concentration of the second jhāna in the fivefold reckoning.

rows.pali: Yo hi vitakkamatteyeva ādīnavaṃ disvā vicāre adisvā kevalaṃ vitakkappahānamattaṃ ākaṅkhamāno paṭhamajjhānaṃ atikkamati, so avitakkavicāramattaṃ samādhiṃ paṭilabhati.
rows.english: For when a man sees danger only in applied thought and not in sustained thought, he aspires only to abandon applied thought when he passes beyond the first jhāna, and so he obtains concentration without applied thought and with sustained thought only.

rows.pali: Taṃ sandhāyetaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: This is said with reference to him.

rows.pali: Catukkanaye pana dutiyādīsu pañcakanaye tatiyādīsu tīsu jhānesu ekaggatā avitakkāvicāro samādhīti evaṃ savitakkasavicārādivasena tividho.
rows.english: Concentration without applied thought and sustained thought is the unification in the three jhānas beginning with the second in the fourfold reckoning and with the third in the fivefold reckoning (see D III 219). So it is of three kinds as with applied thought and sustained thought, and so on.

rows.pali: Tatiyattike catukkanaye ādito dvīsu pañcakanaye ca tīsu jhānesu ekaggatā pītisahagato samādhi.
rows.english: 12. 8. In the third triad concentration accompanied by happiness is the unification in the two first jhānas in the fourfold reckoning and in the three first jhānas in the fivefold reckoning.

rows.pali: Tesveva tatiye ca catutthe ca jhāne ekaggatā sukhasahagato samādhi.
rows.english: Concentration accompanied by bliss is the unification in those same jhānas and in the third and the fourth respectively in the two reckonings.

rows.pali: Avasāne upekkhāsahagato.
rows.english: That accompanied by equanimity is that in the remaining jhāna.

rows.pali: Upacārasamādhi pana pītisukhasahagato vā hoti upekkhāsahagato vāti evaṃ pītisahagatādivasena tividho.
rows.english: Access concentration may be accompanied by bliss and happiness or accompanied by equanimity. So it is of three kinds as accompanied by happiness, and so on.

rows.pali: Catutthattike upacārabhūmiyaṃ ekaggatā paritto samādhi.
rows.english: 13.9.In the fourth triad limited concentration is unification on the plane of access.

rows.pali: Rūpāvacarārūpāvacarakusale ekaggatā mahaggato samādhi.
rows.english: Exalted concentration is unification in profitable [consciousness, etc.,] of the fine- material sphere and immaterial sphere.

rows.pali: Ariyamaggasampayuttā ekaggatā appamāṇo samādhīti evaṃ parittamahaggatappamāṇavasena tividho.
rows.english: Measureless concentration is unification associated with the noble paths. So it is of three kinds as limited, exalted, and measureless.

rows.pali: Catukkesu paṭhamacatukke atthi samādhi dukkhāpaṭipado dandhābhiñño, atthi dukkhāpaṭipado khippābhiñño, atthi sukhāpaṭipado dandhābhiñño, atthi sukhāpaṭipado khippābhiññoti.
rows.english: 14.10. In the first of the tetrads there is concentration of difficult progress and sluggish direct-knowledge. There is that of difficult progress and swift direct- knowledge. There is that of easy progress and sluggish direct-knowledge. And there is that of easy progress and swift direct-knowledge.

rows.pali: Tattha paṭhamasamannāhārato paṭṭhāya yāva tassa tassa jhānassa upacāraṃ uppajjati, tāva pavattā samādhibhāvanā paṭipadāti vuccati.
rows.english: 15.Herein, the development of concentration that occurs from the time of the first conscious reaction up to the arising of the access of a given jhāna is called progress.

rows.pali: Upacārato pana paṭṭhāya yāva appanā, tāva pavattā paññā abhiññāti vuccati.
rows.english: And the understanding that occurs from the time of access until absorption is called direct-knowledge.

rows.pali: Sā panesā paṭipadā ekaccassa dukkhā hoti, nīvaraṇādipaccanīkadhammasamudācāragahaṇatāya kicchā asukhāsevanāti attho.
rows.english: That progress is difficult for some, being troublesome owing to the tenacious resistance of the inimical states beginning with the hindrances. The meaning is that it is cultivated without ease.

rows.pali: Ekaccassa tadabhāvena sukhā.
rows.english: It is easy for others because of the absence of those difficulties.

rows.pali: Abhiññāpi ekaccassa dandhā hoti mandā asīghappavatti.
rows.english: Also the direct-knowledge is sluggish in some and occurs slowly, not quickly.

rows.pali: Ekaccassa khippā amandā sīghappavatti.
rows.english: In others it is swift and occurs rapidly, not slowly.

rows.pali: Tattha yāni parato sappāyāsappāyāni ca palibodhupacchedādīni pubbakiccāni ca appanākosallāni ca vaṇṇayissāma, tesu yo asappāyasevī hoti, tassa dukkhā paṭipadā dandhā ca abhiññā hoti.
rows.english: 16.Herein, we shall comment below upon the suitable and unsuitable (IV.35f.), the preparatory tasks consisting in the severing of impediments (IV.20), etc., and skill in absorption (IV.42). When a man cultivates what is unsuitable, his progress is difficult and his direct-knowledge sluggish.

rows.pali: Sappāyasevino sukhā paṭipadā khippā ca abhiññā.
rows.english: When he cultivates what is suitable, his progress is easy and his direct-knowledge swift.

rows.pali: Yo pana pubbabhāge asappāyaṃ sevitvā aparabhāge sappāyasevī hoti, pubbabhāge vā sappāyaṃ sevitvā aparabhāge asappāyasevī, tassa vomissakatā veditabbā.
rows.english: But if he cultivates the unsuitable in the earlier stage and the suitable in the later stage, or if he cultivates the suitable in the earlier stage and the unsuitable in the later stage, then it should be understood as mixed in his case.

rows.pali: Tathā palibodhupacchedādikaṃ pubbakiccaṃ asampādetvā bhāvanamanuyuttassa dukkhā paṭipadā hoti.
rows.english: Likewise if he devotes himself to development without carrying out the preparatory tasks of severing impediments, etc., his progress is difficult.

rows.pali: Vipariyāyena sukhā.
rows.english: It is easy in the opposite case.

rows.pali: Appanākosallāni pana asampādentassa dandhā abhiññā hoti.
rows.english: And if he is not accomplished in skill in absorption, his direct- knowledge is sluggish.

rows.pali: Sampādentassa khippā.
rows.english: It is swift if he is so accomplished.

rows.pali: Apica taṇhāavijjāvasena samathavipassanādhikāravasena cāpi etāsaṃ pabhedo veditabbo.
rows.english: 17. Besides, they should be understood as classed according to craving and ignorance, and according to whether one has had practice in serenity and insight.7

rows.pali: Taṇhābhibhūtassa hi dukkhā paṭipadā hoti.
rows.english: For if a man is overwhelmed by craving, his progress is difficult.

rows.pali: Anabhibhūtassa sukhā.
rows.english: If not, it is easy.

rows.pali: Avijjābhibhūtassa ca dandhā abhiññā hoti.
rows.english: And if he is overwhelmed by ignorance, his direct-knowledge is sluggish.

rows.pali: Anabhibhūtassa khippā.
rows.english: If not, it is swift.

rows.pali: Yo ca samathe akatādhikāro, tassa dukkhā paṭipadā hoti.
rows.english: And if he has had no practice in serenity, his progress is difficult.

rows.pali: Katādhikārassa sukhā.
rows.english: If he has, it is easy.

rows.pali: Yo pana vipassanāya akatādhikāro hoti, tassa dandhā abhiññā hoti, katādhikārassa khippā.
rows.english: And if he has had no practice in insight, his direct-knowledge is sluggish. If he has, it is swift.

rows.pali: Kilesindriyavasena cāpi etāsaṃ pabhedo veditabbo.
rows.english: 18.Also they should be understood as classed according to defilements and faculties.

rows.pali: Tibbakilesassa hi mudindriyassa dukkhā paṭipadā hoti dandhā ca abhiññā, tikkhindriyassa pana khippā abhiññā.
rows.english: For if a man’s defilements are sharp and his faculties dull, then his progress is difficult and his direct-knowledge sluggish; but if his faculties are keen, his direct-knowledge is swift.

rows.pali: Mandakilesassa ca mudindriyassa sukhā paṭipadā hoti dandhā ca abhiññā.
rows.english: And if his defilements are blunt and his faculties dull, then his progress is easy and his direct-knowledge sluggish;

rows.pali: Tikkhindriyassa pana khippā abhiññāti.
rows.english: but if his faculties are keen, his direct-knowledge is swift.

rows.pali: Iti imāsu paṭipadāabhiññāsu yo puggalo dukkhāya paṭipadāya dandhāya ca abhiññāya samādhiṃ pāpuṇāti, tassa so samādhi dukkhāpaṭipado dandhābhiññoti vuccati.
rows.english: 19.So as regards this progress and this direct-knowledge, when a person reaches concentration with difficult progress and sluggish direct-knowledge, his concentration is called concentration of difficult progress and sluggish direct-knowledge;

rows.pali: Esa nayo sesattayepīti evaṃ dukkhāpaṭipadādandhābhiññādivasena catubbidho.
rows.english: similarly in the cases of the remaining three. So it is of four kinds as of difficult progress and sluggish direct-knowledge, and so on.

rows.pali: Dutiyacatukke atthi samādhi paritto parittārammaṇo, atthi paritto appamāṇārammaṇo, atthi appamāṇo parittārammaṇo, atthi appamāṇo appamāṇārammaṇoti.
rows.english: 20.11. In the second tetrad there is limited concentration with a limited object, there is limited concentration with a measureless object, there is measureless concentration with a limited object, and there is measureless concentration with a measureless object.

rows.pali: Tattha yo samādhi appaguṇo uparijhānassa paccayo bhavituṃ na sakkoti, ayaṃ paritto.
rows.english: Herein, concentration that is unfamiliar and incapable of being a condition for a higher jhāna is limited.

rows.pali: Yo pana avaḍḍhite ārammaṇe pavatto, ayaṃ parittārammaṇo.
rows.english: When it occurs with an unextended object (IV.126), it is with a limited object.

rows.pali: Yo paguṇo subhāvito, uparijhānassa paccayo bhavituṃ sakkoti, ayaṃ appamāṇo.
rows.english: When it is familiar, well developed, and capable of being a condition for a higher jhāna, it is measureless.

rows.pali: Yo ca vaḍḍhite ārammaṇe pavatto, ayaṃ appamāṇārammaṇo.
rows.english: And when it occurs with an extended object, it is with a measureless object.

rows.pali: Vuttalakkhaṇavomissatāya pana vomissakanayo veditabbo.
rows.english: The mixed method can be understood as the mixture of the characteristics already stated.

rows.pali: Evaṃ parittaparittārammaṇādivasena catubbidho.
rows.english: So it is of four kinds as limited with limited object, and so on.

rows.pali: Tatiyacatukke vikkhambhitanīvaraṇānaṃ vitakkavicārapītisukhasamādhīnaṃ vasena pañcaṅgikaṃ paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ, tato vūpasantavitakkavicāraṃ tivaṅgikaṃ dutiyaṃ, tato virattapītikaṃ duvaṅgikaṃ tatiyaṃ, tato pahīnasukhaṃ upekkhāvedanāsahitassa samādhino vasena duvaṅgikaṃ catutthaṃ.
rows.english: 21.12. In the third tetrad the first jhāna has five factors, that is to say, applied thought, sustained thought, happiness, bliss, and concentration, following suppression of the hindrances. The second has the three factors remaining after the elimination of applied and sustained thought. The third has two factors with the fading away of happiness. The fourth, where bliss is abandoned, has two factors with concentration and the equanimous feeling that accompanies it.

rows.pali: Iti imesaṃ catunnaṃ jhānānaṃ aṅgabhūtā cattāro samādhī honti.
rows.english: Thus there are four kinds of concentration according to the factors of these four jhānas.

rows.pali: Evaṃ catujhānaṅgavasena catubbidho.
rows.english: So it is of four kinds according to the factors of the four jhānas.

rows.pali: Catutthacatukke atthi samādhi hānabhāgiyo, atthi ṭhitibhāgiyo, atthi visesabhāgiyo, atthi nibbedhabhāgiyo.
rows.english: 22.13. In the fourth tetrad there is concentration partaking of diminution, there is concentration partaking of stagnation, there is concentration partaking of distinction, and there is concentration partaking of penetration.

rows.pali: Tattha paccanīkasamudācāravasena hānabhāgiyatā, tadanudhammatāya satiyā saṇṭhānavasena ṭhitibhāgiyatā, uparivisesādhigamavasena visesabhāgiyatā, nibbidāsahagatasaññāmanasikārasamudācāravasena nibbedhabhāgiyatā ca veditabbā.
rows.english: Herein, it should be understood that the state of partaking of diminution is accessibility to opposition, the state of partaking of stagnation (ṭhiti) is stationariness (saṇṭhāna) of the mindfulness that is in conformity with that [concentration], the state of partaking of distinction is the attaining of higher distinction, and the state of partaking of penetration is accessibility to perception and attention accompanied by dispassion,

rows.pali: Yathāha, "paṭhamassa jhānassa lābhiṃ kāmasahagatā saññāmanasikārā samudācaranti hānabhāginī paññā.
rows.english: according as it is said: “When a man has attained the first jhāna and he is accessible to perception and attention accompanied by sense desire, then his understanding partakes of diminution.

rows.pali: Tadanudhammatā sati santiṭṭhati ṭhitibhāginī paññā.
rows.english: When his mindfulness that is in conformity with that stagnates, then his understanding partakes of stagnation.

rows.pali: Avitakkasahagatā saññāmanasikārā samudācaranti visesabhāginī paññā.
rows.english: When he is accessible to perception and attention unaccompanied by applied thought, then his understanding partakes of distinction.

rows.pali: Nibbidāsahagatā saññāmanasikārā samudācaranti virāgūpasañhitā nibbedhabhāginī paññā"ti (vibha. 799).
rows.english: When he is accessible to perception and attention accompanied by dispassion and directed to fading away, then his understanding partakes of penetration” (Vibh 330).

rows.pali: Tāya pana paññāya sampayuttā samādhīpi cattāro hontīti.
rows.english: The kinds of concentration associated with that [fourfold] understanding are also four in number.

rows.pali: Evaṃ hānabhāgiyādivasena catubbidho.
rows.english: So it is of four kinds as partaking of diminution, and so on.

rows.pali: Pañcamacatukke kāmāvacaro samādhi, rūpāvacaro samādhi, arūpāvacaro samādhi, apariyāpanno samādhīti evaṃ cattāro samādhī.
rows.english: 23.14. In the fifth tetrad there are the following four kinds of concentration, that is to say, sense-sphere concentration, fine-material-sphere concentration, immaterial- sphere concentration, and unincluded [that is, path] concentration.

rows.pali: Tattha sabbāpi upacārekaggatā kāmāvacaro samādhi.
rows.english: Herein, sense- sphere concentration is all kinds of access unification.

rows.pali: Tathā rūpāvacarādikusalacittekaggatā itare tayoti evaṃ kāmāvacarādivasena catubbidho.
rows.english: Likewise the other three are respectively profitable unification of mind associated with fine-material, [immaterial, and path, jhāna]. So it is of four kinds as of the sense-sphere, and so on.

rows.pali: Chaṭṭhacatukke "chandaṃ ce bhikkhu adhipatiṃ karitvā labhati samādhiṃ, labhati cittassekaggataṃ, ayaṃ vuccati chandasamādhi - pe - vīriyaṃ ce bhikkhu - pe - cittaṃ ce bhikkhu - pe - vīmaṃsaṃ ce bhikkhu adhipatiṃ karitvā labhati samādhiṃ, labhati cittassekaggataṃ, ayaṃ vuccati vīmaṃsāsamādhī"ti (vibha. 432; saṃ. ni. 3.825) evaṃ adhipativasena catubbidho.
rows.english: 24.15. In the sixth tetrad: “If a bhikkhu obtains concentration, obtains unification of mind, by making zeal (desire) predominant, this is called concentration due to zeal. If … by making energy predominant … If … by making [natural purity of] consciousness predominant… If … by making inquiry predominant, this is called concentration due to inquiry” (Vibh 216–19). So it is of four kinds as predominance.

rows.pali: Pañcakeyaṃ catukkabhede vuttaṃ dutiyaṃ jhānaṃ, taṃ vitakkamattātikkamena dutiyaṃ, vitakkavicārātikkamena tatiyanti evaṃ dvidhā bhinditvā pañca jhānāni veditabbāni.
rows.english: 25.16. In the pentad there are five jhānas by dividing in two what is called the second jhāna in the fourfold reckoning (see §21), taking the second jhāna to be due to the surmounting of only applied thought and the third jhāna to be due to the surmounting of both applied and sustained thought. There are five kinds of concentration according to the factors of these five jhānas.

rows.pali: Tesaṃ aṅgabhūtā ca pañca samādhīti evaṃ pañcajhānaṅgavasena pañcavidhatā veditabbā.
rows.english: So its fivefoldness should be understood according to the five sets of jhāna factors.

rows.pali: 40.Ko cassa saṃkileso kiṃ vodānanti ettha pana vissajjanaṃ vibhaṅge vuttameva.
rows.english: 26.(v) What is its defilement? (vi) What is its cleansing?

rows.pali: Vuttañhi tattha "saṃkilesanti hānabhāgiyo dhammo.
rows.english: Here the answer is given in the Vibhaṅga: “Defilement is the state partaking of diminution,

rows.pali: Vodānanti visesabhāgiyo dhammo"ti (vibha. 828).
rows.english: cleansing is the state partaking of distinction” (Vibh 343).

rows.pali: Tattha "paṭhamassa jhānassa lābhiṃ kāmasahagatā saññāmanasikārā samudācaranti hānabhāginī paññā"ti (vibha. 799) iminā nayena hānabhāgiyadhammo veditabbo.
rows.english: Herein, the state partaking of diminution should be understood in this way: “When a man has attained the first jhāna and he is accessible to perception and attention accompanied by sense desire, then his understanding partakes of diminution” (Vibh 330).

rows.pali: "Avitakkasahagatā saññāmanasikārā samudācaranti visesabhāginī paññā"ti (vibha. 799) iminā nayena visesabhāgiyadhammo veditabbo.
rows.english: And the state partaking of distinction should be understood in this way: “When he is accessible to perception and attention unaccompanied by applied thought, then his understanding partakes of distinction” (Vibh 330).

rows.pali: 41.Kathaṃ bhāvetabboti ettha pana yo tāva ayaṃ lokiyalokuttaravasena duvidhotiādīsu ariyamaggasampayutto samādhi vutto, tassa bhāvanānayo paññābhāvanānayeneva saṅgahito.
rows.english: 27.(vii) How should it be developed? The method of developing the kind of concentration associated with the noble paths mentioned (§7) under that “of two kinds as mundane and supramundane,” etc., is included in the method of developing understanding; (Ch. XXII)

rows.pali: Paññāya hi bhāvitāya so bhāvito hoti.
rows.english: for in developing [path] understanding that is developed too.

rows.pali: Tasmā taṃ sandhāya evaṃ bhāvetabboti na kiñci visuṃ vadāma.
rows.english: So we shall say nothing separately [here] about how that is to be developed.

rows.pali: Yo panāyaṃ lokiyo, so vuttanayena sīlāni visodhetvā suparisuddhe sīle patiṭṭhitena yvāssa dasasu palibodhesu palibodho atthi, taṃ upacchinditvā kammaṭṭhānadāyakaṃ kalyāṇamittaṃ upasaṅkamitvā attano cariyānukūlaṃ cattālīsāya kammaṭṭhānesu aññataraṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ gahetvā samādhibhāvanāya ananurūpaṃ vihāraṃ pahāya anurūpe vihāre viharantena khuddakapalibodhupacchedaṃ katvā sabbaṃ bhāvanāvidhānaṃ aparihāpentena bhāvetabboti ayamettha saṅkhepo.
rows.english: 28.But mundane concentration should be developed by one who has taken his stand on virtue that is quite purified in the way already stated. He should sever any of the ten impediments that he may have. He should then approach the good friend, the giver of a meditation subject, and he should apprehend from among the forty meditation subjects one that suits his own temperament. After that he should avoid a monastery unfavourable to the development of concentration and

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana vitthāro, yaṃ tāva vuttaṃ "yvāssa dasasu palibodhesu palibodho atthi, taṃ upacchinditvā"ti, ettha –
rows.english: 29.The detail is this: Firstly it was said above, he should sever any of the ten impediments that he may have.

rows.pali: Ime dasa palibodhā nāma.
rows.english: Now, the “ten impediments” are (above).

rows.pali: Tattha āvāsoyeva āvāsapalibodho.
rows.english: Herein, the dwelling itself is the “impediment due to the dwelling.”

rows.pali: Esa nayo kulādīsu.
rows.english: So too with the family and so on.

rows.pali: Tattha āvāsoti ekopi ovarako vuccati ekampi pariveṇaṃ sakalopi saṅghārāmo.
rows.english: 30. 1. Herein, a single inner room or a single hut or a whole monastery for the Community is called a dwelling.

rows.pali: Svāyaṃ na sabbasseva palibodho hoti.
rows.english: This is not an impediment for everyone.

rows.pali: Yo panettha navakammādīsu ussukkaṃ vā āpajjati, bahubhaṇḍasannicayo vā hoti, yena kenaci vā kāraṇena apekkhavā paṭibaddhacitto, tasseva palibodho hoti, na itarassa.
rows.english: It is an impediment only for anyone whose mind is exercised about the building, etc., that goes on there, or who has many belongings stored there, or whose mind is caught up by some business connected with it. For any other it is not an impediment.

rows.pali: Tatridaṃ vatthu – dve kira kulaputtā anurādhapurā nikkhamitvā anupubbena thūpārāme pabbajiṃsu.
rows.english: 31. Here is a relevant story. Two clansmen left Anurādhapura, it seems, and eventually went forth at the Thūpārāma.8

rows.pali: Tesu eko dve mātikā paguṇā katvā pañcavassiko hutvā pavāretvā pācinakhaṇḍarājiṃ nāma gato.
rows.english: One of them made himself familiar with the Two Codes,9 and when he had acquired five years’ seniority, he took part in the Pavāraṇā10 and then left for the place called Pācīnakhaṇḍarājī.11

rows.pali: Eko tattheva vasati.
rows.english: The other stayed on where he was.

rows.pali: Pācinakhaṇḍarājigato tattha ciraṃ vasitvā thero hutvā cintesi "paṭisallānasāruppamidaṃ ṭhānaṃ, handa naṃ sahāyakassāpi ārocemī"ti.
rows.english: Now, when the one who had gone to Pācīnakhaṇḍarājī had lived there a long time and had become an elder,12 he thought, “This place is good for retreat; suppose I told my friend about it?”

rows.pali: Tato nikkhamitvā anupubbena thūpārāmaṃ pāvisi.
rows.english: So he set out, and in due course he entered the Thūpārāma.

rows.pali: Pavisantaṃyeva ca naṃ disvā samānavassikatthero paccuggantvā pattacīvaraṃ paṭiggahetvā vattaṃ akāsi.
rows.english: As he entered, the elder of the same seniority saw him, went to meet him, took his bowl and robe and did the duties.

rows.pali: Āgantukatthero senāsanaṃ pavisitvā cintesi "idāni me sahāyo sappiṃ vā phāṇitaṃ vā pānakaṃ vā pesessati.
rows.english: 32.The visiting elder went into his lodging. He thought, “Now my friend will be sending me ghee or molasses or a drink;

rows.pali: Ayañhi imasmiṃ nagare ciranivāsī"ti.
rows.english: for he has lived long in this city.”

rows.pali: So rattiṃ aladdhā pāto cintesi "idāni upaṭṭhākehi gahitaṃ yāgukhajjakaṃ pesessatī"ti.
rows.english: He got nothing that night, and in the morning he thought, “Now he will be sending me rice gruel and solid food sent by his supporters.”

rows.pali: Tampi adisvā "pahiṇantā natthi, paviṭṭhassa maññe dassatī"ti pātova tena saddhiṃ gāmaṃ pāvisi.
rows.english: When he saw none, he thought, “There is no one to bring it. No doubt they will give it when we go into the town.” Early in the morning they went into the town together.

rows.pali: Te dve ekaṃ vīthiṃ caritvā uḷuṅkamattaṃ yāguṃ labhitvā āsanasālāyaṃ nisīditvā piviṃsu.
rows.english: When they had wandered through one street and had got only a ladleful of gruel, they sat down in a sitting hall to drink it.13

rows.pali: Tato āgantuko cintesi "nibaddhayāgu maññe natthi, bhattakāle idāni manussā paṇītaṃ bhattaṃ dassantī"ti, tato bhattakālepi piṇḍāya caritvā laddhameva bhuñjitvā itaro āha – "kiṃ, bhante, sabbakālaṃ evaṃ yāpethā"ti?
rows.english: 33.Then the visitor thought, “Perhaps there is no individual giving of gruel. But as soon as it is the time for the meal people will give special food.” But when it was time for the meal, they ate what they had got by wandering for alms. Then the visitor said, “Venerable sir, how is this? Do you live in this way all the time?

rows.pali: Āmāvusoti.
rows.english: ”— “Yes, friend.”

rows.pali: Bhante, pācinakhaṇḍarāji phāsukā, tattha gacchāmāti.
rows.english: —”Venerable sir, Pācīnakhaṇḍarājī is comfortable; let us go there.”

rows.pali: Thero nagarato dakkhiṇadvārena nikkhamanto kumbhakāragāmamaggaṃ paṭipajji.
rows.english: Now, as the elder came out from the city by the southern gate he took the Kumbhakāragāma road [which leads to Pācīnakhaṇḍarājī].

rows.pali: Itaro āha – "kiṃ pana, bhante, imaṃ maggaṃ paṭipannatthā"ti?
rows.english: The visitor asked, “But, venerable sir, why do you take this road?

rows.pali: Nanu tvamāvuso, pācinakhaṇḍarājiyā vaṇṇaṃ abhāsīti?
rows.english: ”—”Did you not recommend Pācīnakhaṇḍarājī, friend?

rows.pali: Kiṃ pana, bhante, tumhākaṃ ettakaṃ kālaṃ vasitaṭṭhāne na koci atirekaparikkhāro atthīti?
rows.english: ”—”But how is this, venerable sir, have you no extra belongings in the place you have lived in for so long?

rows.pali: Āmāvuso mañcapīṭhaṃ saṅghikaṃ, taṃ paṭisāmitameva, aññaṃ kiñci natthīti.
rows.english: ”—”That is so, friend. The bed and chair belong to the Community, and they are put away [as usual]. There is nothing else.”

rows.pali: Mayhaṃ pana, bhante, kattaradaṇḍo telanāḷi upāhanatthavikā ca tatthevāti.
rows.english: —”But, venerable sir, I have left my staff and my oil tube and my sandal bag there.”

rows.pali: Tayāvuso, ekadivasaṃ vasitvā ettakaṃ ṭhapitanti?
rows.english: —”Have you already collected so much, friend, living there for just one day?

rows.pali: Āma, bhante.
rows.english: ”—“Yes, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: So pasannacitto theraṃ vanditvā "tumhādisānaṃ, bhante, sabbattha araññavāsoyeva.
rows.english: 34.He was glad in his heart, and he paid homage to the elder: “For those like you, venerable sir, everywhere is a forest dwelling.

rows.pali: Thūpārāmo catunnaṃ buddhānaṃ dhātunidhānaṭṭhānaṃ, lohapāsāde sappāyaṃ dhammassavanaṃ mahācetiyadassanaṃ theradassanañca labbhati, buddhakālo viya pavattati.
rows.english: The Thūpārāma is a place where the relics of four Buddhas are deposited; there is suitable hearing of the Dhamma in the Brazen Palace; there is the Great Shrine to be seen; and one can visit elders. It is like the time of the Buddha.

rows.pali: Idheva tumhe vasathā"ti dutiyadivase pattacīvaraṃ gahetvā sayameva agamāsīti.
rows.english: It is here that you should live.” On the following day he took his bowl and [outer] robe and went away by himself.

rows.pali: Īdisassa āvāso na palibodho hoti.
rows.english: It is no impediment for one like that.

rows.pali: Kulanti ñātikulaṃ vā upaṭṭhākakulaṃ vā.
rows.english: 35.2 Family means a family consisting of relatives or of supporters.

rows.pali: Ekaccassa hi upaṭṭhākakulampi "sukhitesu sukhito"tiādinā (vibha. 888; saṃ. ni. 4.241) nayena saṃsaṭṭhassa viharato palibodho hoti, so kulamānusakehi vinā dhammassavanāya sāmantavihārampi na gacchati.
rows.english: For even a family consisting of supporters is an impediment for someone who lives in close association with it in the way beginning, “He is pleased when they are pleased” (S III 11), and who does not even go to a neighbouring monastery to hear the Dhamma without members of the family.

rows.pali: Ekaccassa mātāpitaropi palibodhā na honti, koraṇḍakavihāravāsittherassa bhāgineyyadaharabhikkhuno viya.
rows.english: 36.But even mother and father are not an impediment for another, as in the case of the young bhikkhu, the nephew of the elder who lived at the Koraṇḍaka Monastery.

rows.pali: So kira uddesatthaṃ rohaṇaṃ agamāsi.
rows.english: He went to Rohaṇa for instruction, it seems.

rows.pali: Therabhaginīpi upāsikā sadā theraṃ tassa pavattiṃ pucchati.
rows.english: The elder’s sister, who was a lay devotee, was always asking the elder how her son was getting on.

rows.pali: Thero ekadivasaṃ daharaṃ ānessāmīti rohaṇābhimukho pāyāsi.
rows.english: One day the elder set out for Rohaṇa to fetch him back.

rows.pali: Daharopi "ciraṃ me idha vutthaṃ, upajjhāyaṃ dāni passitvā upāsikāya ca pavattiṃ ñatvā āgamissāmī"ti rohaṇato nikkhami.
rows.english: 37.The young bhikkhu too thought, “I have lived here for a long time. Now I might go and visit my preceptor and find out how the lay devotee is,” and he left Rohaṇa.

rows.pali: Te ubhopi gaṅgātīre samāgacchiṃsu.
rows.english: The two met on the banks of the [Mahaveli] River.

rows.pali: So aññatarasmiṃ rukkhamūle therassa vattaṃ katvā "kuhiṃ yāsī"ti pucchito tamatthaṃ ārocesi.
rows.english: He did the duties to the elder at the foot of a tree. When asked, “Where are you going? ” he told him his purpose.

rows.pali: Thero suṭṭhu te kataṃ, upāsikāpi sadā pucchati, ahampi etadatthameva āgato, gaccha tvaṃ, ahaṃ pana idheva imaṃ vassaṃ vasissāmīti taṃ uyyojesi.
rows.english: The elder said: “You have done well. The lay devotee is always asking after you. That was why I came. You may go, but I shall stay here for the Rains,” and he dismissed him.

rows.pali: So vassūpanāyikadivaseyeva taṃ vihāraṃ patto.
rows.english: He arrived at the monastery on the actual day for taking up residence for the Rains.

rows.pali: Senāsanampissa pitarā kāritameva pattaṃ.
rows.english: The lodging allotted to him happened to be the one for which his father had undertaken responsibility.

rows.pali: Athassa pitā dutiyadivase āgantvā "kassa, bhante, amhākaṃ senāsanaṃ patta"nti pucchanto "āgantukassa daharassā"ti sutvā taṃ upasaṅkamitvā vanditvā āha – "bhante, amhākaṃ senāsane vassaṃ upagatassa vattaṃ atthī"ti.
rows.english: 38.His father came on the following day and asked, “To whom was our lodging allotted, venerable sirs? ” When he heard that it had fallen to a young visitor, he went to him. After paying homage to him, he said, “Venerable sir, there is an obligation for him who has taken up residence for the Rains in our lodging.”

rows.pali: Kiṃ upāsakāti?
rows.english: — ”What is it, lay follower?

rows.pali: Temāsaṃ amhākaṃyeva ghare bhikkhaṃ gahetvā pavāretvā gamanakāle āpucchitabbanti.
rows.english: ”—”It is to take alms food only in our house for the three months, and to let us know the time of departure after the Pavāraṇā ceremony.”

rows.pali: So tuṇhibhāvena adhivāsesi.
rows.english: He consented in silence.

rows.pali: Upāsakopi gharaṃ gantvā "amhākaṃ āvāse eko āgantuko ayyo upagato sakkaccaṃ upaṭṭhātabbo"ti āha.
rows.english: The lay devotee went home and told his wife. “There is a visiting lord who has taken up residence for the Rains in our lodging. He must be carefully looked after,”

rows.pali: Upāsikā "sādhū"ti sampaṭicchitvā paṇītaṃ khādanīyaṃ bhojanīyaṃ paṭiyādesi.
rows.english: and she agreed. She prepared good food of various kinds for him.14

rows.pali: Daharopi bhattakāle ñātigharaṃ agamāsi.
rows.english: Though the youth went to his relatives’ home at the time of the meal,

rows.pali: Na naṃ koci sañjāni.
rows.english: no one recognized him.

rows.pali: So temāsampi tattha piṇḍapātaṃ paribhuñjitvā vassaṃvuttho "ahaṃ gacchāmī"ti āpucchi.
rows.english: 39. When he had eaten alms food there during the three months and had completed the residence for the Rains, he announced his departure.

rows.pali: Athassa ñātakā "sve, bhante, gacchathā"ti dutiyadivase ghareyeva bhojetvā telanāḷiṃ pūretvā ekaṃ guḷapiṇḍaṃ navahatthañca sāṭakaṃ datvā "gacchatha, bhante"ti āhaṃsu.
rows.english: Then his relatives said, “Let it be tomorrow, venerable sir,” and on the following day, when they had fed him in their house and filled his oil tube and given him a lump of sugar and a nine-cubit length of cloth, they said, “Now you are leaving, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: So anumodanaṃ katvā rohaṇābhimukho pāyāsi.
rows.english: He gave his blessing and set out for Rohaṇa.

rows.pali: Upajjhāyopissa pavāretvā paṭipathaṃ āgacchanto pubbe diṭṭhaṭṭhāneyeva taṃ addasa.
rows.english: 40.His preceptor had completed the Pavāraṇā ceremony and was on his way back. They met at the same place as before.

rows.pali: So aññatarasmiṃ rukkhamūle therassa vattaṃ akāsi.
rows.english: He did the duties to the elder at the foot of a tree.

rows.pali: Atha naṃ thero pucchi "kiṃ, bhaddamukha, diṭṭhā te upāsikā"ti?
rows.english: The elder asked him, “How was it, my dear, did you see the good woman lay devotee?

rows.pali: So "āma, bhante"ti sabbaṃ pavattiṃ ārocetvā tena telena therassa pāde makkhetvā guḷena pānakaṃ katvā tampi sāṭakaṃ therasseva datvā theraṃ vanditvā "mayhaṃ, bhante, rohaṇaṃyeva sappāya"nti agamāsi.
rows.english: ” He replied, “Yes, venerable sir,” and he told him all that had happened. He then anointed the elder’s feet with the oil, made him a drink with the sugar, and presented him with the length of cloth. He then, after paying homage to the elder, told him, “Venerable sir, only Rohaṇa suits me,” and he departed.

rows.pali: Theropi vihāraṃ āgantvā dutiyadivase koraṇḍakagāmaṃ pāvisi.
rows.english: The elder too arrived back at his monastery, and next day he went into the village of Koraṇḍaka.

rows.pali: Upāsikāpi "mayhaṃ bhātā mama puttaṃ gahetvā idāni āgacchatī"ti sadā maggaṃ olokayamānāva tiṭṭhati.
rows.english: 41.The lay devotee, his sister, had always kept looking down the road, thinking, “My brother is now coming with my son.”

rows.pali: Sā taṃ ekakameva āgacchantaṃ disvā "mato me maññe putto, ayaṃ thero ekakova āgacchatī"ti therassa pādamūle nipatitvā paridevamānā rodi.
rows.english: When she saw him coming alone, she thought, “My son must be dead; that is why the elder is coming alone,” and she fell at the elder’s feet, lamenting and weeping.

rows.pali: Thero "nūna daharo appicchatāya attānaṃ ajānāpetvāva gato"ti taṃ samassāsetvā sabbaṃ pavattiṃ ārocetvā pattatthavikato taṃ sāṭakaṃ nīharitvā dasseti.
rows.english: Suspecting that it must have been out of fewness of wishes that the youth had gone away without announcing himself, the elder comforted her and told her all that had happened, and he took the length of cloth out of his bag and showed it to her.

rows.pali: Upāsikā pasīditvā puttena gatadisābhimukhā urena nipajjitvā namassamānā āha – "mayhaṃ puttasadisaṃ vata maññe bhikkhuṃ kāyasakkhiṃ katvā bhagavā rathavinītapaṭipadaṃ (ma. ni. 1.252 ādayo), nālakapaṭipadaṃ (su. ni. 684 ādayo), tuvaṭṭakapaṭipadaṃ (su. ni. 921 ādayo), catupaccayasantosabhāvanārāmatādīpakaṃ mahāariyavaṃsapaṭipadañca (a. ni. 4.28; dī. ni. 3.309) desesi.
rows.english: 42.She was appeased. She prostrated herself in the direction taken by her son, and she said: “Surely the Blessed One taught the way of the Rathavinīta, the way of the Nālaka, the way of the Tuvaṭaka, and the way of the great Noble Ones’ heritages15 showing contentment with the four requisites and delight in development, making a bhikkhu such as my son a body-witness.

rows.pali: Vijātamātuyā nāma gehe temāsaṃ bhuñjamānopi 'ahaṃ putto tvaṃ mātā'ti na vakkhati, aho acchariyamanusso"ti.
rows.english: So, although for three months he ate in the house of the mother who bore him, yet he never said ‘I am your son, you are my mother!’ Oh, admirable man!

rows.pali: Evarūpassa mātāpitaropi palibodhā na honti, pageva aññaṃ upaṭṭhākakula"nti.
rows.english: ” Even mother and father are no impediment for one such as him, so how much less any other family that supports him.

rows.pali: Lābhoti cattāro paccayā.
rows.english: 43. 3. Gain is the four requisites.

rows.pali: Te kathaṃ palibodhā honti?
rows.english: How are they an impediment?

rows.pali: Puññavantassa hi bhikkhuno gatagataṭṭhāne manussā mahāparivāre paccaye denti.
rows.english: Wherever a meritorious bhikkhu goes, people give him a large supply of requisites.

rows.pali: So tesaṃ anumodento dhammaṃ desento samaṇadhammaṃ kātuṃ na okāsaṃ labhati.
rows.english: With giving blessings to them and teaching them the Dhamma he gets no chance to do the ascetic’s duties.

rows.pali: Aruṇuggamanato yāva paṭhamayāmo, tāva manussasaṃsaggo na upacchijjati.
rows.english: From sunrise till the first watch of the night he never breaks his association with people.

rows.pali: Puna balavapaccūseyeva bāhullikapiṇḍapātikā āgantvā "bhante, asuko upāsako upāsikā amacco amaccadhītā tumhākaṃ dassanakāmā"ti vadanti, so gaṇhāvuso, pattacīvaranti gamanasajjova hotīti niccabyāvaṭo, tasseva te paccayā palibodhā honti.
rows.english: Again, even at dawn, alms-food eaters fond of opulence come and say, “Venerable sir, such and such a man lay follower, woman lay follower, friend, friend’s daughter, wants to see you,” and being ready to go, he replies, “Take the bowl and robe, friend.” So he is always on the alert. Thus these requisites are an impediment for him.

rows.pali: Tena gaṇaṃ pahāya yattha naṃ na jānanti, tattha ekakena caritabbaṃ.
rows.english: He should leave his group and wander by himself where he is not known.

rows.pali: Evaṃ so palibodho upacchijjatīti.
rows.english: This is the way his impediment is severed.

rows.pali: Gaṇoti suttantikagaṇo vā ābhidhammikagaṇo vā, yo tassa uddesaṃ vā paripucchaṃ vā dento samaṇadhammassa okāsaṃ na labhati, tasseva gaṇo palibodho hoti, tena so evaṃ upacchinditabbo.
rows.english: 44.4 Class is a class (group) of students of suttas or students of Abhidhamma. If with the group’s instruction and questioning he gets no opportunity for the ascetic’s duties, then that group is an impediment for him. He should sever that impediment in this way:

rows.pali: Sace tesaṃ bhikkhūnaṃ bahu gahitaṃ hoti, appaṃ avasiṭṭhaṃ, taṃ niṭṭhapetvā araññaṃ pavisitabbaṃ.
rows.english: if those bhikkhus have already acquired the main part and little still remains, he should finish that off and then go to the forest.

rows.pali: Sace appaṃ gahitaṃ, bahu avasiṭṭhaṃ, yojanato paraṃ agantvā antoyojanaparicchede aññaṃ gaṇavācakaṃ upasaṅkamitvā "ime āyasmā uddesādīhi saṅgaṇhatū"ti vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: If they have only acquired little and much still remains, he should, without travelling more than a league, approach another instructor of a class within the radius of a league and say, “Help those venerable ones with instruction, etc.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ alabhamānena "mayhamāvuso, ekaṃ kiccaṃ atthi, tumhe yathāphāsukaṭṭhānāni gacchathā"ti gaṇaṃ pahāya attano kammaṃ kattabbanti.
rows.english: If he does not find anyone in this way, he should take leave of the class, saying. “I have a task to see to, friends; go where it suits you,” and he should do his own work.

rows.pali: Kammanti navakammaṃ.
rows.english: 45.5. Building (kamma) is new building work (nava-kamma).

rows.pali: Taṃ karontena vaḍḍhakīādīhi laddhāladdhaṃ jānitabbaṃ, katākate ussukkaṃ āpajjitabbanti sabbadā palibodho hoti.
rows.english: Since one engaged in this must know about what [material] has and has not been got by carpenters, etc., and must see about what has and has not been done, it is always an impediment.

rows.pali: Sopi evaṃ upacchinditabbo, sace appaṃ avasiṭṭhaṃ hoti niṭṭhapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: It should be severed in this way. If little remains it should be completed.

rows.pali: Sace bahu, saṅghikañce navakammaṃ, saṅghassa vā saṅghabhārahārakabhikkhūnaṃ vā niyyādetabbaṃ.
rows.english: If much remains, it should be handed over to the Community or to bhikkhus who are entrusted with the Community’s affairs, if it is a new building for the Community;

rows.pali: Attano santakañce, attano bhārahārakānaṃ niyyādetabbaṃ.
rows.english: or if it is for himself, it should be handed over to those whom he entrusts with his own affairs,

rows.pali: Tādise alabhantena saṅghassa pariccajitvā gantabbanti.
rows.english: but if these are not available, he should relinquish it to the Community and depart.

rows.pali: Addhānanti maggagamanaṃ.
rows.english: 46.6. Travel is going on a journey.

rows.pali: Yassa hi katthaci pabbajjāpekkho vā hoti, paccayajātaṃ vā kiñci laddhabbaṃ hoti.
rows.english: If someone is expected to give the going forth somewhere else, or if some requisite is obtainable there

rows.pali: Sace taṃ alabhanto na sakkoti adhivāsetuṃ, araññaṃ pavisitvā samaṇadhammaṃ karontassapi gamikacittaṃ nāma duppaṭivinodanīyaṃ hoti, tasmā gantvā taṃ kiccaṃ tīretvāva samaṇadhamme ussukkaṃ kātabbanti.
rows.english: and he cannot rest content without getting it [that will be an impediment; for] even if he goes into the forest to do the ascetic’s duties, he will find it hard to get rid of thoughts about the journey. So one in this position should apply himself to the ascetic’s duties after he has done the journey and transacted the business.

rows.pali: Ñātīti vihāre ācariyupajjhāyasaddhivihārikaantevāsikasamānupajjhāyakasamānācariyakā, ghare mātā pitā bhātāti evamādikā.
rows.english: 47.7 Kin in the case of the monastery means teacher, preceptor, co-resident, pupil, those with the same preceptor as oneself, and those with the same teacher as oneself; and in the case of the house it means mother, father, brother, and so on.

rows.pali: Te gilānā imassa palibodhā honti, tasmā so palibodho upaṭṭhahitvā tesaṃ pākatikakaraṇena upacchinditabbo.
rows.english: When they are sick they are an impediment for him. Therefore that impediment should be severed by curing them with nursing.

rows.pali: Tattha upajjhāyo tāva gilāno sace lahuṃ na vuṭṭhāti, yāvajīvampi paṭijaggitabbo.
rows.english: 48.Herein, when the preceptor is sick he must be cared for as long as life lasts if the sickness does not soon depart.

rows.pali: Tathā pabbajjācariyo upasampadācariyo saddhivihāriko upasampāditapabbājitaantevāsikasamānupajjhāyakā ca.
rows.english: Likewise the teacher at the going forth, the teacher at the admission, the co-resident, the pupils to whom one has given the admission and the going forth, and those who have the same preceptor.

rows.pali: Nissayācariyauddesācariyanissayantevāsikauddesantevāsikasamānācariyakā pana yāva nissayauddesā anupacchinnā, tāva paṭijaggitabbā.
rows.english: But the teacher from whom one takes the dependence, the teacher who gives one instruction, the pupil to whom one has given the dependence, the pupil to whom one is giving instruction, and those who have that same teacher as oneself, should be looked after as long as the dependence or the instruction has not been terminated.

rows.pali: Pahontena tato uddhampi paṭijaggitabbā eva.
rows.english: If one is able to do so, one should look after them even beyond that [period].

rows.pali: Mātāpitūsu upajjhāye viya paṭipajjitabbaṃ.
rows.english: 49.Mother and father should be treated like the preceptor;

rows.pali: Sacepi hi te rajje ṭhitā honti, puttato ca upaṭṭhānaṃ paccāsīsanti, kātabbameva.
rows.english: if they live within the kingdom and look to their son for help, it should be given.

rows.pali: Atha tesaṃ bhesajjaṃ natthi, attano santakaṃ dātabbaṃ.
rows.english: Also if they have no medicine, he should give them his own.

rows.pali: Asati bhikkhācariyāya pariyesitvāpi dātabbameva.
rows.english: If he has none, he should go in search of it as alms and give that.

rows.pali: Bhātubhaginīnaṃ pana tesaṃ santakameva yojetvā dātabbaṃ.
rows.english: But in the case of brothers or sisters, one should only give them what is theirs.

rows.pali: Sace natthi attano santakaṃ tāvakālikaṃ datvā pacchā labhantena gaṇhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: If they have none, then one should give one’s own temporarily and later get it back,

rows.pali: Alabhantena na codetabbā.
rows.english: but one should not complain if one does not get it back.

rows.pali: Aññātakassa bhaginisāmikassa bhesajjaṃ neva kātuṃ na dātuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: It is not allowed either to make medicine for or to give it to a sister’s husband who is not related by blood;

rows.pali: "Tuyhaṃ sāmikassa dehī"ti vatvā pana bhaginiyā dātabbaṃ.
rows.english: but one can give it to one’s sister saying, “Give it to your husband.”

rows.pali: Bhātujāyāyapi eseva nayo.
rows.english: The same applies to one’s brother’s wife.

rows.pali: Tesaṃ pana puttā imassa ñātakā evāti tesaṃ kātuṃ vaṭṭatīti.
rows.english: But it is allowed to make it for their children since they are blood relatives.

rows.pali: Ābādhoti yokoci rogo.
rows.english: 50.8. Affliction is any kind of illness.

rows.pali: So bādhayamāno palibodho hoti, tasmā bhesajjakaraṇena upacchinditabbo.
rows.english: It is an impediment when it is actually afflicting; therefore it should be severed by treatment with medicine.

rows.pali: Sace pana katipāhaṃ bhesajjaṃ karontassapi na vūpasammati, nāhaṃ tuyhaṃ dāso, na bhaṭako, taṃyeva hi posento anamatagge saṃsāravaṭṭe dukkhaṃ pattoti attabhāvaṃ garahitvā samaṇadhammo kātabboti.
rows.english: But if it is not cured after taking medicine for a few days, then the ascetic’s duties should be done after apostrophizing one’s person in this way: “I am not your slave, or your hireling. I have come to suffering through maintaining you through the beginningless round of rebirths.”

rows.pali: Ganthoti pariyattiharaṇaṃ.
rows.english: 51. 9. Books means responsibility for the scriptures.

rows.pali: Taṃ sajjhāyādīhi niccabyāvaṭassa palibodho hoti, na itarassa.
rows.english: That is an impediment only for one who is constantly busy with recitations, etc., but not for others.

rows.pali: Tatrimāni vatthūni –
rows.english: Here are relevant stories.

rows.pali: Majjhimabhāṇakadevatthero kira malayavāsidevattherassa santikaṃ gantvā kammaṭṭhānaṃ yāci.
rows.english: The Elder Revata, it seems, the Majjhima reciter, went to the Elder Revata, the dweller in Malaya (the Hill Country), and asked him for a meditation subject.

rows.pali: Thero kīdisosi, āvuso, pariyattiyanti pucchi.
rows.english: The elder asked him, “How are you in the scriptures, friend?

rows.pali: Majjhimo me, bhante, paguṇoti.
rows.english: ”—”I am studying the Majjhima [Nikāya], venerable sir.”

rows.pali: Āvuso, majjhimo nāmeso dupparihāro, mūlapaṇṇāsaṃ sajjhāyantassa majjhimapaṇṇāsako āgacchati, taṃ sajjhāyantassa uparipaṇṇāsako.
rows.english: —”The Majjhima is a hard responsibility, friend. When a man is still learning the First Fifty by heart, he is faced with the Middle Fifty; and when he is still learning that by heart, he is faced with the Last Fifty.

rows.pali: Kuto tuyhaṃ kammaṭṭhānanti?
rows.english: How can you take up a meditation subject?”

rows.pali: Bhante, tumhākaṃ santike kammaṭṭhānaṃ labhitvā puna na olokessāmīti kammaṭṭhānaṃ gahetvā ekūnavīsativassāni sajjhāyaṃ akatvā vīsatime vasse arahattaṃ patvā sajjhāyatthāya āgatānaṃ bhikkhūnaṃ "vīsati me, āvuso, vassāni pariyattiṃ anolokentassa, apica kho kataparicayo ahamettha ārabhathā"ti vatvā ādito paṭṭhāya yāva pariyosānā ekabyañjanepissa kaṅkhā nāhosi.
rows.english: —”Venerable sir, when I have taken a meditation subject from you, I shall not look at the scriptures again.” He took the meditation subject, and doing no recitation for nineteen years, he reached Arahantship in the twentieth year. He told bhikkhus who came for recitation: “I have not looked at the scriptures for twenty years, friends, yet I am familiar with them. You may begin.” And from beginning to end he had no hesitation even over a single syllable.

rows.pali: Karuḷiyagirivāsīnāgattheropi aṭṭhārasavassāni pariyattiṃ chaḍḍetvā bhikkhūnaṃ dhātukathaṃ uddisi.
rows.english: 52.The Elder Mahā-Nāga, too, who lived at Karuliyagiri (Karaliyagiri) put aside the scriptures for eighteen years, and then he recited the Dhātukathā to the bhikkhus.

rows.pali: Tesaṃ gāmavāsikattherehi saddhiṃ saṃsandentānaṃ ekapañhopi uppaṭipāṭiyā āgato nāhosi.
rows.english: When they checked this with the town-dwelling elders [of Anurādha- pura], not a single question was found out of its order.

rows.pali: Mahāvihārepi tipiṭakacūḷābhayatthero nāma aṭṭhakathaṃ anuggahetvāva pañcanikāyamaṇḍale tīṇi piṭakāni parivattessāmīti suvaṇṇabheriṃ paharāpesi.
rows.english: 53.In the Great Monastery too the Elder Tipiṭaka-Cūḷa-Abhaya had the golden drum struck, saying: “I shall expound the three Piṭakas in the circle of [experts in] the Five Collections of discourses,” and this was before he had learnt the commentaries.

rows.pali: Bhikkhusaṅgho katamācariyānaṃ uggaho, attano ācariyuggahaññeva vadatu, itarathā vattuṃ na demāti āha.
rows.english: The Community of Bhikkhus said, “‘Which teachers’ teaching is it? Unless you give only the teaching of our own teachers we shall not let you speak.”

rows.pali: Upajjhāyopi naṃ attano upaṭṭhānamāgataṃ pucchi "tvamāvuso, bheriṃ paharāpesī"ti?
rows.english: Also his preceptor asked him when he went to wait on him, “Did you have the drum beaten, friend?

rows.pali: Āma, bhante.
rows.english: ”—”Yes, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: Kiṃ kāraṇāti?
rows.english: —”For what reason?

rows.pali: Pariyattiṃ, bhante, parivattessāmīti.
rows.english: ”—”I shall expound the scriptures, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: Āvuso abhaya, ācariyā idaṃ padaṃ kathaṃ vadantīti?
rows.english: —”Friend Abhaya, how do the teachers explain this passage?

rows.pali: Evaṃ vadanti, bhanteti.
rows.english: ”—”They explain it in this way, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: Thero hunti paṭibāhi.
rows.english: The elder dissented, saying “Hum.”

rows.pali: Puna so aññena aññena pariyāyena evaṃ vadanti bhanteti tikkhattuṃ āha.
rows.english: Again three times, each time in a different way, he said, “They explain it in this way, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: Thero sabbaṃ hunti paṭibāhitvā "āvuso, tayā paṭhamaṃ kathito eva ācariyamaggo, ācariyamukhato pana anuggahitattā 'evaṃ ācariyā vadantī'ti saṇṭhātuṃ nāsakkhi.
rows.english: The elder always dissented, saying, “Hum.” Then he said, “Friend, your first explanation was the way of the teachers. But it is because you have not actually learnt it from the teachers’ lips that you are unable to maintain that the teachers say such and such.

rows.pali: Gaccha attano ācariyānaṃ santike suṇāhī"ti.
rows.english: Go and learn it from our own teachers.”

rows.pali: Kuhiṃ, bhante, gacchāmīti?
rows.english: —”Where shall I go, venerable sir?

rows.pali: Gaṅgāya parato rohaṇajanapade tulādhārapabbatavihāre sabbapariyattiko mahādhammarakkhitatthero nāma vasati, tassa santikaṃ gacchāti.
rows.english: ”—”There is an elder named Mahā Dhammarakkhita living in the Tulādhārapabbata Monastery in the Rohaṇa country beyond the [Mahaveli] River. He knows all the scriptures. Go to him.”

rows.pali: Sādhu, bhanteti theraṃ vanditvā pañcahi bhikkhusatehi saddhiṃ therassa santikaṃ gantvā vanditvā nisīdi.
rows.english: Saying, “Good, venerable sir,” he paid homage to the elder. He went with five hundred bhikkhus to the Elder Mahā-Dhammarakkhita, and when he had paid homage to him, he sat down.

rows.pali: Thero kasmā āgatosīti pucchi.
rows.english: The elder asked, “Why have you come?

rows.pali: Dhammaṃ sotuṃ, bhanteti.
rows.english: ”—”To hear the Dhamma, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: Āvuso abhaya, dīghamajjhimesu maṃ kālena kālaṃ pucchanti.
rows.english: —”Friend Abhaya, they ask me about the Dīgha and the Majjhima from time to time,

rows.pali: Avasesaṃ pana me tiṃsamattāni vassāni na olokitapubbaṃ.
rows.english: but I have not looked at the others for thirty years.

rows.pali: Apica tvaṃ rattiṃ mama santike parivattehi.
rows.english: Still you may repeat them in my presence by night,

rows.pali: Ahaṃ te divā kathayissāmīti.
rows.english: and I shall explain them to you by day.”

rows.pali: So sādhu, bhanteti tathā akāsi.
rows.english: He said, “Good, venerable sir,” and he acted accordingly.

rows.pali: Pariveṇadvāre mahāmaṇḍapaṃ kāretvā gāmavāsino divase divase dhammassavanatthāya āgacchanti.
rows.english: 54.The inhabitants of the village had a large pavilion built at the door of his dwelling, and they came daily to hear the Dhamma.

rows.pali: Thero rattiṃ parivatti.
rows.english: what had been repeated by night

rows.pali: Taṃ divā kathayanto anupubbena desanaṃ niṭṭhapetvā abhayattherassa santike taṭṭikāya nisīditvā "āvuso, mayhaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ kathehī"ti āha.
rows.english: explaining by day ,the Elder [Dhammarakkhita] eventually completed the instruction. Then he sat down on a mat on the ground before the Elder Abhaya and said, “Friend, explain a meditation subject to me.”

rows.pali: Bhante, kiṃ bhaṇatha, nanu mayā tumhākameva santike sutaṃ?
rows.english: —”What are you saying, venerable sir, have I not heard it all from you?

rows.pali: Kimahaṃ tumhehi aññātaṃ kathessāmīti?
rows.english: What can I explain to you that you do not already know?

rows.pali: Tato naṃ thero añño esa, āvuso, gatakassa maggo nāmāti āha.
rows.english: ” The senior elder said, “This path is different for one who has actually travelled by.”

rows.pali: Abhayathero kira tadā sotāpanno hoti.
rows.english: 55.The Elder Abhaya was then, it seems, a stream-enterer.

rows.pali: Athassa so kammaṭṭhānaṃ datvā āgantvā lohapāsāde dhammaṃ parivattento thero parinibbutoti assosi.
rows.english: When the Elder Abhaya had given his teacher a meditation subject, he returned to Anurādhapura. Later, while he was expounding the Dhamma in the Brazen Palace, he heard that the elder had attained Nibbāna.

rows.pali: Sutvā "āharathāvuso, cīvara"nti cīvaraṃ pārupitvā "anucchaviko, āvuso, amhākaṃ ācariyassa arahattamaggo.
rows.english: On hearing this, he said, “Bring me [my] robe, friends.” Then he put on the robe and said, “The Arahant path befits our teacher, friends.

rows.pali: Ācariyo no, āvuso, uju ājānīyo.
rows.english: Our teacher was a true thoroughbred.

rows.pali: So attano dhammantevāsikassa santike taṭṭikāya nisīditvā 'mayhaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ kathehī'ti āha.
rows.english: He sat down on a mat before his own Dhamma pupil and said, ‘Explain a meditation subject to me.’

rows.pali: Anucchaviko, āvuso, therassa arahattamaggo"ti.
rows.english: The Arahant path befits our teacher, friends.”

rows.pali: Evarūpānaṃ gantho palibodho na hotīti.
rows.english: For such as these, books are no impediment.

rows.pali: Iddhīti pothujjanikā iddhi.
rows.english: 56. 10. Supernormal powers are the supernormal powers of the ordinary man.

rows.pali: Sā hi uttānaseyyakadārako viya taruṇasassaṃ viya ca dupparihārā hoti.
rows.english: They are hard to maintain, like a prone infant or like young corn,

rows.pali: Appamattakeneva bhijjati.
rows.english: and the slightest thing breaks them.

rows.pali: Sā pana vipassanāya palibodho hoti, na samādhissa, samādhiṃ patvā pattabbato.
rows.english: But they are an impediment for insight, not for concentration, since they are obtainable through concentration.

rows.pali: Tasmā vipassanatthikena iddhipalibodho upacchinditabbo, itarena avasesāti ayaṃ tāva palibodhakathāya vitthāro.
rows.english: So the supernormal powers are an impediment that should be severed by one who seeks insight; the others are impediments to be severed by one who seeks concentration. This, in the first place, is the detailed explanation of the impediments.

rows.pali: 42.Kammaṭṭhānadāyakaṃkalyāṇamittaṃ upasaṅkamitvāti ettha pana duvidhaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ sabbatthakakammaṭṭhānaṃ pārihāriyakammaṭṭhānañca.
rows.english: 57.Approach the good friend, the giver of a meditation subject (§28): meditation subjects are of two kinds, that is, generally useful meditation subjects and special meditation subjects.

rows.pali: Tattha sabbatthakakammaṭṭhānaṃ nāma bhikkhusaṅghādīsu mettā maraṇassati ca.
rows.english: Herein, loving-kindness towards the Community of Bhikkhus, etc., and also mindfulness of death are what are called generally useful meditation subjects.

rows.pali: Asubhasaññātipi eke.
rows.english: Some say perception of foulness, too.

rows.pali: Kammaṭṭhānikena hi bhikkhunā paṭhamaṃ tāva paricchinditvā sīmaṭṭhakabhikkhusaṅghe sukhitā hontu abyāpajjāti mettā bhāvetabbā.
rows.english: 58. When a bhikkhu takes up a meditation subject, he should first develop loving-kindness towards the Community of Bhikkhus within the boundary,16 limiting it at first [to “all bhikkhus in this monastery”], in this way: “May they be happy and free from affliction.”

rows.pali: Tato sīmaṭṭhakadevatāsu.
rows.english: Then he should develop it towards all deities within the boundary.

rows.pali: Tato gocaragāmamhi issarajane.
rows.english: Then towards all the principal people in the village that is his alms resort;

rows.pali: Tato tattha manusse upādāya sabbasattesu.
rows.english: then to [all human beings there and to] all living beings dependent on the human beings.

rows.pali: So hi bhikkhusaṅghe mettāya sahavāsīnaṃ muducittataṃ janeti.
rows.english: With loving-kindness towards the Community of Bhikkhus he produces kindliness in his co-residents;

rows.pali: Athassa te sukhasaṃvāsā honti.
rows.english: then they are easy for him to live with.

rows.pali: Sīmaṭṭhakadevatāsu mettāya mudukatacittāhi devatāhi dhammikāya rakkhāya susaṃvihitarakkho hoti.
rows.english: With loving-kindness towards the deities within the boundary he is protected by kindly deities with lawful protection.

rows.pali: Gocaragāmamhi issarajane mettāya mudukatacittasantānehi issarehi dhammikāya rakkhāya surakkhitaparikkhāro hoti.
rows.english: With loving- kindness towards the principal people in the village that is his alms resort his requisites are protected by well-disposed principal people with lawful protection.

rows.pali: Tattha manussesu mettāya pasāditacittehi tehi aparibhūto hutvā vicarati.
rows.english: With loving-kindness to all human beings there he goes about without incurring their dislike since they trust him.

rows.pali: Sabbasattesu mettāya sabbattha appaṭihatacāro hoti.
rows.english: With loving-kindness to all living beings he can wander unhindered everywhere.

rows.pali: Maraṇassatiyā pana avassaṃ mayā maritabbanti cintento anesanaṃ pahāya uparūpari vaḍḍhamānasaṃvego anolīnavuttiko hoti.
rows.english: With mindfulness of death, thinking, “I have got to die,” he gives up improper search (see S II 194; M-a I 115), and with a growing sense of urgency he comes to live without attachment.

rows.pali: Asubhasaññāparicitacittassa panassa dibbānipi ārammaṇāni lobhavasena cittaṃ na pariyādiyanti.
rows.english: When his mind is familiar with the perception of foulness, then even divine objects do not tempt his mind to greed.

rows.pali: Evaṃ bahūpakārattā sabbattha atthayitabbaṃ icchitabbanti ca adhippetassa yogānuyogakammassa ṭhānañcāti sabbatthakakammaṭṭhānanti vuccati.
rows.english: 59.So these are called “generally useful” and they are “called meditation subjects” since they are needed17 generally and desirable owing to their great helpfulness and since they are subjects for the meditation work intended.

rows.pali: Cattālīsāya pana kammaṭṭhānesu yaṃ yassa cariyānukūlaṃ, taṃ tassa niccaṃ pariharitabbattā uparimassa ca uparimassa bhāvanākammassa padaṭṭhānattā pārihāriyakammaṭṭhānanti vuccati.
rows.english: 60.What is called a “special meditation subject” is that one from among the forty meditation subjects that is suitable to a man’s own temperament. It is “special” (pārihāriya) because he must carry it (pariharitabbattā) constantly about with him, and because it is the proximate cause for each higher stage of development.

rows.pali: Iti imaṃ duvidhampi kammaṭṭhānaṃ yo deti, ayaṃ kammaṭṭhānadāyako nāma.
rows.english: So it is the one who gives this twofold meditation subject that is called the giver of a meditation subject.

rows.pali: Taṃ kammaṭṭhānadāyakaṃ.
Kalyāṇamittanti –
rows.english: 61.The good friend

rows.pali: Evamādiguṇasamannāgataṃ ekantena hitesiṃ vuddhipakkhe ṭhitaṃ kalyāṇamittaṃ.
rows.english: (The good friend) is one who possesses such special qualities as these (above). He is wholly solicitous of welfare and partial to progress.

rows.pali: "Mamaṃ hi, ānanda, kalyāṇamittaṃ āgamma jātidhammā sattā jātiyā parimuccantī"ti (saṃ. ni. 1.129; 5.2) ādivacanato pana sammāsambuddhoyeva sabbākārasampanno kalyāṇamitto.
rows.english: 62.Because of the words beginning, “Ānanda, it is owing to my being a good friend to them that living beings subject to birth are freed from birth” (S I 88), it is only the Fully Enlightened One who possesses all the aspects of the good friend.

rows.pali: Tasmā tasmiṃ sati tasseva bhagavato santike gahitakammaṭṭhānaṃ sugahitaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Since that is so, while he is available only a meditation subject taken in the Blessed One’s presence is well taken.

rows.pali: Parinibbute pana tasmiṃ asītiyā mahāsāvakesu yo dharati, tassa santike gahetuṃ vaṭṭati.
rows.english: But after his final attainment of Nibbāna, it is proper to take it from anyone of the eighty great disciples still living.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ asati yaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ gahetukāmo hoti, tasseva vasena catukkapañcakajjhānāni nibbattetvā jhānapadaṭṭhānaṃ vipassanaṃ vaḍḍhetvā āsavakkhayappattassa khīṇāsavassa santike gahetabbaṃ.
rows.english: When they are no more available, one who wants to take a particular meditation subject should take it from someone with cankers destroyed, who has, by means of that particular meditation subject, produced the fourfold and fivefold jhāna, and has reached the destruction of cankers by augmenting insight that had that jhāna as its proximate cause.

rows.pali: Kiṃ pana khīṇāsavo ahaṃ khīṇāsavoti attānaṃ pakāsetīti?
rows.english: 63.But how then, does someone with cankers destroyed declare himself thus: “I am one whose cankers are destroyed?”

rows.pali: Kiṃ vattabbaṃ, kārakabhāvaṃ hi jānitvā pakāseti.
rows.english: Why not? He declares himself when he knows that his instructions will be carried out.

rows.pali: Nanu assaguttatthero āraddhakammaṭṭhānassa bhikkhuno "kammaṭṭhānakārako aya"nti jānitvā ākāse cammakhaṇḍaṃ paññāpetvā tattha pallaṅkena nisinno kammaṭṭhānaṃ kathesīti.
rows.english: Did not the Elder Assagutta spread out his leather mat in the air and sitting cross-legged on it explain a meditation subject to a bhikkhu who was starting his meditation subject, because he knew that that bhikkhu was one who would carry out his instructions for the meditation subject?

rows.pali: Tasmā sace khīṇāsavaṃ labhati, iccetaṃ kusalaṃ, no ce labhati, anāgāmisakadāgāmisotāpannajhānalābhīputhujjanatipiṭakadharadvipiṭakadharaekapiṭakadharesu purimassa purimassa santike.
rows.english: 64.So if someone with cankers destroyed is available, that is good. If not, then one should take it from a non-returner, a once-returner, a stream-enterer, an ordinary man who has obtained jhāna, one who knows three Piṭakas, one who knows two Piṭakas, one who knows one Piṭaka, in descending order [according as available].

rows.pali: Ekapiṭakadharepi asati yassa ekasaṅgītipi aṭṭhakathāya saddhiṃ paguṇā, ayañca lajjī hoti, tassa santike gahetabbaṃ.
rows.english: If not even one who knows one Piṭaka is available, then it should be taken from one who is familiar with one Collection together with its commentary and one who is himself conscientious.

rows.pali: Evarūpo hi tantidharo vaṃsānurakkhako paveṇīpālako ācariyo ācariyamatikova hoti, na attanomatiko hoti.
rows.english: For a teacher such as this, who knows the texts, guards the heritage, and protects the tradition, will follow the teachers’ opinion rather than his own.

rows.pali: Teneva porāṇakattherā "lajjī rakkhissati lajjī rakkhissatī"ti tikkhattuṃ āhaṃsu.
rows.english: Hence the Ancient Elders said three times, “One who is conscientious will guard it.”

rows.pali: Pubbe vuttakhīṇāsavādayo cettha attanā adhigatamaggameva ācikkhanti.
rows.english: 65.Now, those beginning with one whose cankers are destroyed, mentioned above, will describe only the path they have themselves reached.

rows.pali: Bahussuto pana taṃ taṃ ācariyaṃ upasaṅkamitvā uggahaparipucchānaṃ visodhitattā ito cito ca suttañca kāraṇañca sallakkhetvā sappāyāsappāyaṃ yojetvā gahanaṭṭhāne gacchanto mahāhatthī viya mahāmaggaṃ dassento kammaṭṭhānaṃ kathessati.
rows.english: But with a learned man, his instructions and his answers to questions are purified by his having approached such and such teachers, and so he will explain a meditation subject showing a broad track, like a big elephant going through a stretch of jungle, and he will select suttas and reasons from here and there, adding [explanations of] what is suitable and unsuitable.

rows.pali: Tasmā evarūpaṃ kammaṭṭhānadāyakaṃ kalyāṇamittaṃ upasaṅkamitvā tassa vattapaṭipattiṃ katvā kammaṭṭhānaṃ gahetabbaṃ.
rows.english: So a meditation subject should be taken by approaching the good friend such as this, the giver of a meditation subject, and by doing all the duties to him.

rows.pali: Sace panetaṃ ekavihāreyeva labhati, iccetaṃ kusalaṃ, no ce labhati, yattha so vasati, tattha gantabbaṃ.
rows.english: 66.If he is available in the same monastery, it is good. If not, one should go to where he lives.

rows.pali: Gacchantena ca na dhotamakkhitehi pādehi upāhanā ārūhitvā chattaṃ gahetvā telanāḷimadhuphāṇitādīni gāhāpetvā antevāsikaparivutena gantabbaṃ.
rows.english: When [a bhikkhu] goes to him, he should not do so with feet washed and anointed, wearing sandals, with an umbrella, surrounded by pupils, and bringing oil tube, honey, molasses, etc.;

rows.pali: Gamikavattaṃ pana pūretvā attano pattacīvaraṃ sayameva gahetvā antarāmagge yaṃ yaṃ vihāraṃ pavisati sabbattha vattapaṭipattiṃ kurumānena sallahukaparikkhārena paramasallekhavuttinā hutvā gantabbaṃ.
rows.english: he should do so fulfilling the duties of a bhikkhu setting out on a journey, carrying his bowl and robes himself, doing all the duties in each monastery on the way, with few belongings, and living in the greatest effacement.

rows.pali: Taṃ vihāraṃ pavisantena antarāmaggeyeva dantakaṭṭhaṃ kappiyaṃ kārāpetvā gahetvā pavisitabbaṃ, na ca "muhuttaṃ vissametvā pādadhovanamakkhanādīni katvā ācariyassa santikaṃ gamissāmī"ti aññaṃ pariveṇaṃ pavisitabbaṃ.
rows.english: When entering that monastery, he should do so [expecting nothing, and even provided] with a tooth-stick that he has had made allowable on the way [according to the rules]. And he should not enter some other room, thinking, “I shall go to the teacher after resting awhile and after washing and anointing my feet, and so on.”

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: 67.Why?

rows.pali: Sace hissa tatra ācariyassa visabhāgā bhikkhū bhaveyyuṃ, te āgamanakāraṇaṃ pucchitvā ācariyassa avaṇṇaṃ pakāsetvā "naṭṭhosi, sace tassa santikaṃ āgato"ti vippaṭisāraṃ uppādeyyuṃ, yena tatova paṭinivatteyya, tasmā ācariyassa vasanaṭṭhānaṃ pucchitvā ujukaṃ tattheva gantabbaṃ.
rows.english: If there are bhikkhus there who are hostile to the teacher, they might ask him the reason for his coming and speak dispraise of the teacher, saying, “You are done for if you go to him”; they might make him regret his coming and turn him back. So he should ask for the teacher’s dwelling and go straight there.

rows.pali: Sace ācariyo daharataro hoti, pattacīvarapaṭiggahaṇādīni na sāditabbāni.
rows.english: 68.If the teacher is junior, he should not consent to the teacher’s receiving his bowl and robe, and so on.

rows.pali: Sace vuḍḍhataro hoti, gantvā ācariyaṃ vanditvā ṭhātabbaṃ.
rows.english: If the teacher is senior, then he should go and pay homage to him and remain standing.

rows.pali: "Nikkhipāvuso, pattacīvara"nti vuttena nikkhipitabbaṃ.
rows.english: When told, “Put down the bowl and robe, friend,” he may put them down.

rows.pali: "Pānīyaṃ pivā"ti vuttena sace icchati pātabbaṃ.
rows.english: When told, “Have some water to drink,” he can drink if he wants to.

rows.pali: "Pāde dhovāhī"ti vuttena na tāva pādā dhovitabbā.
rows.english: When told, “You may wash your feet,” he should not do so at once,

rows.pali: Sace hi ācariyena ābhataṃ udakaṃ bhaveyya, na sāruppaṃ siyā.
rows.english: for if the water has been brought by the teacher himself, it would be improper.

rows.pali: "Dhovāhāvuso, na mayā ābhataṃ, aññehi ābhata"nti vuttena pana yattha ācariyo na passati, evarūpe paṭicchanne vā okāse, abbhokāse vihārassāpi vā ekamante nisīditvā pādā dhovitabbā.
rows.english: But when told “Wash, friend, it was not brought by me, it was brought by others,” then he can wash his feet, sitting in a screened place out of sight of the teacher, or in the open to one side of the dwelling.

rows.pali: Sace ācariyo telanāḷiṃ āharati uṭṭhahitvā ubhohi hatthehi sakkaccaṃ gahetabbā.
rows.english: 69.If the teacher brings an oil tube, he should get up and take it carefully with both hands.

rows.pali: Sace hi na gaṇheyya, "ayaṃ bhikkhu ito eva paṭṭhāya sambhogaṃ kopetī"ti ācariyassa aññathattaṃ bhaveyya.
rows.english: If he did not take it, it might make the teacher wonder, “Does this bhikkhu resent sharing so soon?

rows.pali: Gahetvā pana na āditova pādā makkhetabbā.
rows.english: ” but having taken it, he should not anoint his feet at once.

rows.pali: Sace hi taṃ ācariyassa gattabbhañjanatelaṃ bhaveyya, na sāruppaṃ siyā.
rows.english: For if it were oil for anointing the teacher’s limbs, it would not be proper.

rows.pali: Tasmā sīsaṃ makkhetvā khandhādīni makkhetabbāni.
rows.english: So he should first anoint his head, then his shoulders, etc.;

rows.pali: "Sabbapārihāriyatelamidaṃ, āvuso, pādepi makkhehī"ti vuttena pana thokaṃ sīse katvā pāde makkhetvā "imaṃ telanāḷiṃ ṭhapemi, bhante"ti vatvā ācariye gaṇhante dātabbā.
rows.english: but when told, “This is meant for all the limbs, friend, anoint your feet,” he should put a little on his head and then anoint his feet. Then he should give it back, saying when the teacher takes it, “May I return this oil tube, venerable sir? ”

rows.pali: Āgatadivasato paṭṭhāya kammaṭṭhānaṃ me, bhante, kathetha iccevaṃ na vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: 70.He should not say, “Explain a meditation subject to me, venerable sir” on the very day he arrives.

rows.pali: Dutiyadivasato pana paṭṭhāya sace ācariyassa pakatiupaṭṭhāko atthi, taṃ yācitvā vattaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: But starting from the next day, he can, if the teacher has a habitual attendant, ask his permission to do the duties.

rows.pali: Sace yācitopi na deti, okāse laddheyeva kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: If he does not allow it when asked, they can be done when the opportunity offers.

rows.pali: Karontena khuddakamajjhimamahantāni tīṇi dantakaṭṭhāni upanāmetabbāni.
rows.english: When he does them, three tooth-sticks should be brought, a small, a medium and a big one,

rows.pali: Sītaṃ uṇhanti duvidhaṃ mukhadhovanaudakañca nhānodakañca paṭiyādetabbaṃ.
rows.english: and two kinds of mouth-washing water and bathing water, that is, hot and cold, should be set out.

rows.pali: Tato yaṃ ācariyo tīṇi divasāni paribhuñjati, tādisameva niccaṃ upanāmetabbaṃ.
rows.english: Whichever of these the teacher uses for three days should then be brought regularly.

rows.pali: Niyamaṃ akatvā yaṃ vā taṃ vā paribhuñjantassa yathāladdhaṃ upanāmetabbaṃ.
rows.english: If the teacher uses either kind indiscriminately, he can bring whatever is available.

rows.pali: Kiṃ bahunā vuttena?
rows.english: 71.Why so many words?

rows.pali: Yaṃ taṃ bhagavatā "antevāsikena, bhikkhave, ācariyamhi sammā vattitabbaṃ.
rows.english: as by the Blessed One : “Bhikkhus, a pupil should perform the duties to the teacher rightly.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ sammā vattanā, kālasseva uṭṭhāya upāhanā omuñcitvā ekaṃsaṃ uttarāsaṅgaṃ karitvā dantakaṭṭhaṃ dātabbaṃ, mukhodakaṃ dātabbaṃ, āsanaṃ paññapetabbaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, this is the right performance of duties. He should rise early; removing his sandals and arranging his robe on one shoulder, he should give the tooth-sticks and the mouth-washing water, and he should prepare the seat.

rows.pali: Sace yāgu hoti, bhājanaṃ dhovitvā yāgu upanāmetabbā"ti (mahāva. 78) ādikaṃ khandhake sammāvattaṃ paññattaṃ, taṃ sabbampi kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: If there is rice gruel, he should wash the dish and bring the rice gruel” (Vin I 61). All should be done as prescribed in the Khandhakas as the right duties in the passage beginning (above).

rows.pali: Evaṃ vattasampattiyā garuṃ ārādhayamānena sāyaṃ vanditvā yāhīti vissajjitena gantabbaṃ, yadā so kissāgatosīti pucchati, tadā āgamanakāraṇaṃ kathetabbaṃ.
rows.english: 72.To please the teacher by perfection in the duties he should pay homage in the evening, and he should leave when dismissed with the words, “You may go.” When the teacher asks him, “Why have you come?” he can explain the reason for his coming.

rows.pali: Sace so neva pucchati, vattaṃ pana sādiyati, dasāhe vā pakkhe vā vītivatte ekadivasaṃ vissajjitenāpi agantvā okāsaṃ kāretvā āgamanakāraṇaṃ ārocetabbaṃ.
rows.english: If he does not ask but agrees to the duties being done, then after ten days or a fortnight have gone by he should make an opportunity by staying back one day at the time of his dismissal, and announcing the reason for his coming;

rows.pali: Akāle vā gantvā kimatthamāgatosīti puṭṭhena ārocetabbaṃ.
rows.english: or he should go at an unaccustomed time, and when asked, “What have you come for? ” he can announce it.

rows.pali: Sace so pātova āgacchāti vadati, pātova gantabbaṃ.
rows.english: 73.If the teacher says, “Come in the morning,” he should do so.

rows.pali: Sace panassa tāya velāya pittābādhena vā kucchi pariḍayhati, aggimandatāya vā bhattaṃ na jīrati, añño vā koci rogo bādhati, taṃ yathābhūtaṃ āvikatvā attano sappāyavelaṃ ārocetvā tāya velāya upasaṅkamitabbaṃ.
rows.english: But if his stomach burns with a bile affliction at that hour, or if his food does not get digested owing to sluggish digestive heat, or if some other ailment afflicts him, he should let it be known, and proposing a time that suits himself, he should come at that time.

rows.pali: Asappāyavelāya hi vuccamānampi kammaṭṭhānaṃ na sakkā hoti manasikātunti.
rows.english: For if a meditation subject is expounded at an inconvenient time, one cannot give attention.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ kammaṭṭhānadāyakaṃ kalyāṇamittaṃ upasaṅkamitvāti ettha vitthāro.
rows.english: This is the detailed explanation of the words “approach the good friend, the giver of a meditation subject.”

rows.pali: 43.Idāni attano cariyānukūlanti ettha cariyāti cha cariyā rāgacariyā, dosacariyā, mohacariyā, saddhācariyā, buddhicariyā, vitakkacariyāti.
rows.english: 74.Now, as to the words, one that suits his temperament (§28): there are six kinds of temperament, that is, greedy temperament, hating temperament, deluded temperament, faithful temperament, intelligent temperament, and speculative temperament.

rows.pali: Keci pana rāgādīnaṃ saṃsaggasannipātavasena aparāpi catasso, tathā saddhādīnanti imāhi aṭṭhahi saddhiṃ cuddasa icchanti.
rows.english: Some would have fourteen, taking these six single ones together with the four made up of the three double combinations and one triple combination with the greed triad and likewise with the faith triad.

rows.pali: Evaṃ pana bhede vuccamāne rāgādīnaṃ saddhādīhipi saṃsaggaṃ katvā anekā cariyā honti, tasmā saṅkhepena chaḷeva cariyā veditabbā.
rows.english: But if this classification is admitted, there are many more kinds of temperament possible by combining greed, etc., with faith, etc.; therefore the kinds of temperament should be understood briefly as only six.

rows.pali: Cariyā, pakati, ussannatāti atthato ekaṃ.
rows.english: As to meaning the temperaments are one, that is to say, personal nature, idiosyncrasy.

rows.pali: Tāsaṃ vasena chaḷeva puggalā honti rāgacarito, dosacarito, mohacarito, saddhācarito, buddhicarito, vitakkacaritoti.
rows.english: According to [102] these there are only six types of persons, that is, one of greedy temperament, one of hating temperament, one of deluded temperament, one of faithful temperament, one of intelligent temperament, and one of speculative temperament.

rows.pali: Tattha yasmā rāgacaritassa kusalappavattisamaye saddhā balavatī hoti, rāgassa āsannaguṇattā.
rows.english: 75.Herein, one of faithful temperament is parallel to one of greedy temperament because faith is strong when profitable [kamma] occurs in one of greedy temperament, owing to its special qualities being near to those of greed.

rows.pali: Yathā hi akusalapakkhe rāgo siniddho nātilūkho, evaṃ kusalapakkhe saddhā.
rows.english: For, in an unprofitable way, greed is affectionate and not over-austere, and so, in a profitable way, is faith.

rows.pali: Yathā rāgo vatthukāme pariyesati, evaṃ saddhā sīlādiguṇe.
rows.english: Greed seeks out sense desires as object, while faith seeks out the special qualities of virtue and so on.

rows.pali: Yathā rāgo ahitaṃ na pariccajati, evaṃ saddhā hitaṃ na pariccajati, tasmā rāgacaritassa saddhācarito sabhāgo.
rows.english: And greed does not give up what is harmful, while faith does not give up what is beneficial.

rows.pali: Yasmā pana dosacaritassa kusalappavattisamaye paññā balavatī hoti, dosassa āsannaguṇattā.
rows.english: 76.One of intelligent temperament is parallel to one of hating temperament because understanding is strong when profitable [kamma] occurs in one of hating temperament, owing to its special qualities being near to those of hate.

rows.pali: Yathā hi akusalapakkhe doso nissineho na ārammaṇaṃ allīyati, evaṃ kusalapakkhe paññā.
rows.english: For, in an unprofitable way, hate is disaffected and does not hold to its object, and so, in a profitable way, is understanding.

rows.pali: Yathā ca doso abhūtampi dosameva pariyesati, evaṃ paññā bhūtaṃ dosameva.
rows.english: Hate seeks out only unreal faults, while understanding seeks out only real faults.
